slides.title: The 60MB Payload Challenge
slides.content: When dealing with 60,000 slides, we move from manual prompting to 'Content Factory' architecture. The total payload size is approximately 60MB—a significant volume for JSON-based manifests. This scale introduces extreme entropy; a 0.1% failure rate in the model's output formatting would result in 60 broken files. The primary engineering challenge is ensuring strict schema adherence across 120 individual 500-slide manifests while managing API rate limits and token budgets. Orchestration must handle context distribution: each slide needs access to global taxonomy and styling rules, but repeating 50KB of context for every slide would be a 'Denial of Wallet' event. Strategizing the balance between context injection and sliding-window generation is the key to successfully delivering the FRAISE 60MB capstone payload without exceeding reasonable operational costs.
slides.svgContent: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f8fafc'/><rect x='50' y='50' width='60' height='80' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#3b82f6'/><rect x='60' y='60' width='60' height='80' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.3' stroke='#3b82f6'/><rect x='70' y='70' width='60' height='80' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.4' stroke='#3b82f6'/><text x='150' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' font-family='sans-serif' fill='#1e40af'>Massive Serialization Strategy (120 Files)</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Why is it better to split 60k slides into 120 files rather than one giant 60MB JSON?
How would you handle a 'Schema Failure' halfway through a 1,200-request generation run?
What is the impact of JSON verbosity (keys like 'svgContent') on total payload delivery time?

slides.title: Total Request & Throughput Calculation
slides.content: To generate 60,000 slides, we must calculate the 'Request Efficiency.' Given our constraint of 500 slides per manifest (JSON limit), we need exactly 120 files. However, generating 500 cohesive slides in a SINGLE request often leads to model fatigue or truncation. Strategy (A): 120 Requests (Fastest, High Risk). Strategy (B): Batching 50 slides per request = 1,200 total requests (Balanced). Strategy (C): 10 slides per request = 6,000 requests (Highest Quality, High Latency). For the FRAISE Capstone, Strategy B (1,200 requests) provides the best balance. Total Time Calculation: If Gemini Flash handles 10 concurrent requests at 1.5s/request, the 1,200 requests complete in ~180 seconds. This 'Parallel Throughput' is essential for keeping development cycles tight even when bulk-generating massive educational structures.
slides.svgContent: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M30 150 L270 150 M30 150 L30 30' stroke='gray' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M30 150 Q100 140 120 100 T270 30' fill='none' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='3'/><text x='150' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='#991b1b'>Wait Time vs Batch Size</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Calculate the total time needed if you are strictly rate-limited to 60 requests per minute (RPM).
Explain the 'Model Fatigue' concept: why does quality degrade as the output token length approaches the model's limit?
How would you design a 'Resume' logic for a 1,200-request script that crashes at request 943?

slides.title: Token Cost Optimization (Context Caching)
slides.content: The 60,000-slide project is a 'Repeat Context' scenario. Every request needs the same System Instructions (~1k tokens) and the Project Taxonomy (~4k tokens). Total input token overhead: 5,000 tokens x 1,200 requests = 6,000,000 tokens. Using 'Context Caching' (Gemini API Feature), you cache the 5k tokens for a small hourly fee, paying only the 'Input Cache Hit' rate (significantly cheaper). Estimated Output: Each slide is ~1KB (including SVG). 60,000 slides = 60M tokens generated. Pricing Strategy: Using Gemini 1.5 Flash (approx $0.15/1M input, $0.60/1M output), the project costs ~$36 in output tokens and <$1 in input tokens with caching. Without caching, input costs would swell to ~$10+. Mastery of these pricing vectors allows you to build features that are financially viable at massive scale.
slides.svgContent: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='50' y='50' width='200' height='100' rx='10' fill='#f0fdf4' stroke='#16a34a'/><path d='M70 150 H230' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2'/><text x='150' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='#166534'>Caching Savings: 80% Reduction in Input Costs</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Compare the cost of Flash vs Pro for this 60M token output task. Is the quality difference worth a 10x cost increase?
Explain the tradeoff between TTL (Time To Live) in a cache and the total generation time.
How do you monitor your token quota in real-time to avoid a mid-project 'Quota Exceeded' failure?

slides.title: Tiered Quality Control
slides.content: A successful 60MB payload delivery requires 'Tiered Verification.' Strategy: (1) Generate structural bones with Gemini 1.5 Flash. (2) Use a Lightweight Schema Validator to catch broken JSON. (3) Identify the 'Power Slides' (1% of the total) that require high-precision visual logic or complex math. (4) Re-submit only those slides to Gemini 1.5 Pro for refinement. This hybrid approach ensures 60,000 slides are structurally sound and cost-effective, while the most important pedagogical units maintain top-tier quality. By treating AI as a component in a pipeline rather than a magic box, you achieve Enterprise-grade stability for the FRAISE internal training ecosystem.
slides.svgContent: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='150,20 280,180 20,180' fill='#fefce8' stroke='#ca8a04'/><line x1='80' y1='100' x2='220' y2='100' stroke='#ca8a04'/><text x='150' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='#854d0e'>The Quality Pyramid: Flash (Base) -> Pro (Peak)</text></svg>
